name: Jesus Christ
bio: The central figure of all scripture, recognized as the Son of God and Savior of the world. In the Old Testament, he is the God of Israel (Jehovah) who covenanted with Abraham, led Israel from Egypt, and spoke through prophets. In the New Testament, he was born of Mary in Bethlehem, ministered in Galilee and Judea, was crucified, and rose from the dead. The Book of Mormon records his appearance to the Nephites after his resurrection.

name: God the Father
bio: The supreme deity and father of all spirits, including Jesus Christ. He spoke from heaven at Jesus' baptism and on the Mount of Transfiguration. In the Pearl of Great Price, he is called Man of Holiness. He appeared with Jesus Christ to Joseph Smith in the First Vision of 1820.

name: Holy Ghost
bio: The third member of the Godhead, a personage of spirit. He testifies of the Father and the Son, reveals truth, and provides comfort and guidance. He descended upon Jesus at his baptism in the form of a dove and fell upon the Nephite disciples at the time of Christ's visit.

name: Adam
bio: The first man, created by God and placed in the Garden of Eden with Eve. After the Fall, he and Eve were taught the gospel and offered sacrifices. In LDS theology, he is also the archangel Michael who helped create the earth. He held a council at Adam-ondi-Ahman before his death.

name: Eve
bio: The first woman, formed from Adam's rib and placed in the Garden of Eden. She partook of the forbidden fruit and, with Adam, was expelled from the garden. She is recognized as the mother of all living. The Pearl of Great Price records that she and Adam received the gospel and rejoiced in God's plan of redemption.

name: Noah
bio: A patriarch who was commanded by God to build an ark to save his family and animals from the great flood. He preached repentance to a wicked generation for 120 years before the flood came. After the flood, God established a covenant with Noah symbolized by the rainbow. In LDS theology, he is identified with the angel Gabriel.

name: Abraham
bio: The great patriarch originally named Abram, called by God to leave Ur of the Chaldees. God made a covenant with him promising innumerable posterity and a land of inheritance. He was willing to sacrifice his son Isaac at God's command. The Book of Abraham in the Pearl of Great Price records his teachings on astronomy and the pre-mortal existence.

name: Sarah
bio: Wife of Abraham, originally named Sarai. She was barren for many years until God promised she would bear a son. She gave birth to Isaac in her old age. Her name was changed from Sarai to Sarah, meaning 'princess,' as a sign of God's covenant.

name: Isaac
bio: Son of Abraham and Sarah, born miraculously in their old age. As a young man, he was willingly offered as a sacrifice by his father Abraham on Mount Moriah before an angel intervened. He married Rebekah and became the father of twins Esau and Jacob. He is the second of the three great patriarchs of Israel.

name: Rebekah
bio: Wife of Isaac and mother of twins Esau and Jacob. She was the daughter of Bethuel and granddaughter of Nahor, Abraham's brother. She received a revelation that the elder (Esau) would serve the younger (Jacob) and helped Jacob receive Isaac's blessing.

name: Jacob
bio: Son of Isaac and Rebekah, twin brother of Esau. He received the birthright and blessing from his father and was later renamed Israel by God after wrestling with an angel at Peniel. He married Leah and Rachel and fathered twelve sons who became the heads of the twelve tribes of Israel.

name: Esau
bio: Firstborn twin son of Isaac and Rebekah, brother of Jacob. He sold his birthright to Jacob for a meal of lentil stew and later lost his father's blessing. He became the ancestor of the Edomites. Despite initial anger at Jacob, the two brothers eventually reconciled.

name: Joseph
bio: Eleventh son of Jacob and firstborn of Rachel. Sold into Egypt by his jealous brothers, he rose from slavery to become second-in-command to Pharaoh through his God-given ability to interpret dreams. He saved his family from famine and is a significant type of Christ. The Book of Mormon especially honors his lineage, as Lehi's family descended from him through Manasseh.

name: Moses
bio: Prophet who led the children of Israel out of Egyptian bondage. He received the Ten Commandments and the law on Mount Sinai, parted the Red Sea, and guided Israel for forty years in the wilderness. He is the author of the Pentateuch. In LDS scripture, the Book of Moses (Pearl of Great Price) records additional visions given to him, and he appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration and in the Kirtland Temple to restore priesthood keys.

name: Aaron
bio: Older brother of Moses and first high priest of Israel. He served as Moses' spokesman before Pharaoh and assisted in leading Israel out of Egypt. He was consecrated as high priest and his descendants held the Aaronic Priesthood. He made the golden calf while Moses was on Sinai, which brought God's displeasure.

name: Miriam
bio: Elder sister of Moses and Aaron. She watched over the infant Moses in his basket on the Nile and helped arrange for his mother to nurse him. After the crossing of the Red Sea, she led the women of Israel in a song of triumph. She was stricken with leprosy for speaking against Moses but was healed after seven days.

name: Joshua
bio: Successor to Moses who led the Israelites across the Jordan River into the promised land of Canaan. He commanded the conquest of Jericho and led Israel in the wars to take possession of the land. Before his death, he gathered Israel at Shechem and declared, 'Choose you this day whom ye will serve... but as for me and my house, we will serve the Lord.'

name: David
bio: Shepherd, musician, warrior, and second king of Israel. He slew Goliath as a youth, was anointed by Samuel, and eventually became king over all Israel. He conquered Jerusalem and made it his capital. He was called a man after God's own heart, though he committed serious sins including adultery with Bathsheba and arranging the death of her husband Uriah.

name: Solomon
bio: Son of David and Bathsheba, third king of Israel. Known for his extraordinary wisdom, which he received as a gift from God. He built the first Temple in Jerusalem and expanded Israel's wealth and influence. In later life, he turned to idolatry through the influence of his many foreign wives.

name: Isaiah
bio: One of the greatest Old Testament prophets, who ministered in Judah during the reigns of Uzziah, Jotham, Ahaz, and Hezekiah (c. 740–700 BC). His prophecies of the Messiah are among the most detailed in scripture. Large portions of Isaiah are quoted in the Book of Mormon, particularly by Nephi and by Jesus Christ himself.

name: Jeremiah
bio: Prophet in Judah who was called as a youth, ministering from the reign of Josiah through the fall of Jerusalem to Babylon (c. 626–586 BC). He warned of Jerusalem's destruction and was persecuted for his prophecies. He was a contemporary of Lehi and is mentioned in the Book of Mormon. Tradition says he was taken to Egypt after Jerusalem fell.

name: Ezekiel
bio: A priest and prophet who was among the Jews taken captive to Babylon in 597 BC. He prophesied from Babylon, receiving dramatic visions including the valley of dry bones (symbolizing Israel's restoration) and a detailed vision of a future temple. His prophecy of the sticks of Judah and Joseph is interpreted as foreshadowing the Bible and Book of Mormon.

name: Daniel
bio: A young nobleman from Judah taken captive to Babylon. He served in the courts of Nebuchadnezzar and later Darius, interpreting dreams and receiving visions of future kingdoms and the last days. He survived the lions' den through faith. His apocalyptic visions parallel those in Revelation.

name: Elijah
bio: A powerful prophet of Israel during the reign of King Ahab. He challenged the priests of Baal on Mount Carmel, was fed by ravens, raised a widow's son, and was taken to heaven in a chariot of fire without tasting death. He appeared on the Mount of Transfiguration with Moses and appeared in the Kirtland Temple in 1836 to restore the sealing keys of the priesthood.

name: Elisha
bio: Prophet of Israel who succeeded Elijah after receiving a double portion of his spirit. He performed many miracles including healing Naaman the Syrian of leprosy, multiplying a widow's oil, raising the Shunammite woman's son, and feeding a hundred men with twenty loaves. He served during the reigns of several kings of Israel.

name: Samuel
bio: Last of the judges and first of the prophets after Moses in the traditional sense. His mother Hannah dedicated him to the Lord's service from birth. He anointed both Saul and David as kings of Israel. He served as judge, prophet, and priest, and his leadership marked the transition from the period of judges to the monarchy.

name: Saul
bio: First king of Israel, anointed by Samuel. He was tall and initially humble but became disobedient to God's commands. He grew jealous of David and tried to kill him. He consulted the witch of Endor before his final battle and died by falling on his own sword on Mount Gilboa after being wounded by Philistine archers.

name: Jonathan
bio: Son of King Saul and devoted friend of David. Despite his father's jealousy of David, Jonathan remained fiercely loyal to David and helped protect him from Saul's attempts on his life. He was a brave warrior who died alongside his father in battle against the Philistines on Mount Gilboa.

name: Ruth
bio: A Moabite woman who married into an Israelite family and, after her husband's death, chose to stay with her mother-in-law Naomi rather than return to Moab. She famously declared, 'Whither thou goest, I will go.' She married Boaz and became the great-grandmother of King David, placing her in the lineage of Jesus Christ.

name: Esther
bio: A Jewish woman who became queen of Persia as wife of King Ahasuerus (Xerxes). She risked her life to save the Jewish people from a genocide plot by Haman. Her story is celebrated in the Jewish festival of Purim. Her courage is captured in Mordecai's challenge: 'Who knoweth whether thou art come to the kingdom for such a time as this?'

name: Enoch
bio: A pre-flood patriarch who walked with God and was translated (taken to heaven without dying). In the Pearl of Great Price, he is a major figure: a prophet who built the city of Zion, whose people were so righteous that the entire city was taken up to heaven. He saw a sweeping vision of all human history and wept over the wickedness of the world.

name: Cain
bio: Firstborn son of Adam and Eve. He murdered his brother Abel after God accepted Abel's offering but rejected his own. When confronted by God, he asked, 'Am I my brother's keeper?' He was cursed to be a fugitive and wanderer. In the Pearl of Great Price, he is portrayed as entering a covenant with Satan.

name: Abel
bio: Second son of Adam and Eve, a keeper of sheep. He offered a righteous sacrifice that was accepted by God, which provoked his brother Cain to murder him. He is referenced in the New Testament as a man of faith and the first martyr.

name: Seth
bio: Third named son of Adam and Eve, born after Abel's death. He was in the likeness of Adam and became the progenitor of the line leading to Noah. The Pearl of Great Price describes him as a righteous man who was ordained by Adam.

name: Methuselah
bio: Son of Enoch and grandfather of Noah. He is famous as the longest-lived person in the Bible at 969 years. He is mentioned in the lineage from Adam to Noah. In the Pearl of Great Price, he received prophecies from his father Enoch.

name: Leah
bio: Older daughter of Laban and first wife of Jacob, given to him through Laban's deception. Though described as having 'tender eyes' and overshadowed by Jacob's love for Rachel, she bore six of Jacob's twelve sons (Reuben, Simeon, Levi, Judah, Issachar, Zebulun) and a daughter Dinah.

name: Rachel
bio: Younger daughter of Laban and beloved wife of Jacob. Jacob served Laban fourteen years to marry her. After years of barrenness, she gave birth to Joseph and later died giving birth to Benjamin near Bethlehem. She is an important matriarch whose descendants (through Joseph) include the Nephites and Lamanites.

name: Judah
bio: Fourth son of Jacob and Leah. He persuaded his brothers to sell Joseph into slavery rather than kill him. He later pledged his own life for Benjamin's safety. He became the ancestor of the tribe of Judah, from which came King David and Jesus Christ. The Southern Kingdom of Israel took his name.

name: Hagar
bio: Egyptian handmaid of Sarah and mother of Ishmael by Abraham. When Sarah was barren, she gave Hagar to Abraham as a concubine. After conflict with Sarah, Hagar was twice sent away into the wilderness, where an angel ministered to her and promised that her son Ishmael would father a great nation.

name: Ishmael
bio: Son of Abraham and Hagar. He was circumcised at age thirteen as part of God's covenant with Abraham. God promised he would become a great nation with twelve princes. He and his mother were sent away by Abraham at Sarah's insistence but were preserved by God in the wilderness. He is regarded as an ancestor of the Arab peoples.

name: Lot
bio: Nephew of Abraham who traveled with him from Ur to Canaan. He chose to dwell in the Jordan plain near Sodom and was captured in a war before being rescued by Abraham. Angels warned him to flee before Sodom's destruction; his wife looked back and became a pillar of salt.

name: Deborah
bio: A prophetess and the only female judge of Israel recorded in the Old Testament. She held court under a palm tree and directed Barak to lead Israel's army against the Canaanite general Sisera. After the victory, she composed a song of triumph (Judges 5), one of the oldest poems in the Bible.

name: Gideon
bio: A judge of Israel whom God called to deliver the Israelites from the Midianites. An angel appeared to him, and God confirmed his call through the sign of the fleece. With only 300 men chosen through a specific test, he routed a vast Midianite army using trumpets and torches, demonstrating God's power.

name: Samson
bio: A judge of Israel who was dedicated as a Nazirite from birth and possessed supernatural strength. He fought the Philistines through various exploits but was betrayed by Delilah, who discovered the secret of his strength was his uncut hair. After being blinded and imprisoned, he brought down the Philistine temple in a final act of strength.

name: Hannah
bio: Wife of Elkanah who was barren for many years. She fervently prayed at the tabernacle in Shiloh for a son, vowing to dedicate him to the Lord. Her prayer was answered with the birth of Samuel, whom she brought to serve under Eli the priest. Her prayer of thanksgiving (1 Samuel 2) echoes themes later found in Mary's Magnificat.

name: Eli
bio: High priest and judge of Israel at Shiloh who mentored the young Samuel. He was a well-meaning but weak leader whose sons Hophni and Phinehas were corrupt priests. God pronounced judgment against his house, and Eli died upon hearing that the Ark of the Covenant had been captured by the Philistines.

name: Bathsheba
bio: Wife of Uriah the Hittite and later wife of King David, who saw her bathing and committed adultery with her. David arranged Uriah's death in battle to conceal the affair. After the death of their first child, she bore Solomon, who became king of Israel. She later advocated for Solomon's succession.

name: Absalom
bio: Third son of King David, known for his beauty and his rebellion against his father. He killed his half-brother Amnon for violating his sister Tamar, fled into exile, and later led a revolt that temporarily drove David from Jerusalem. He was killed by Joab while hanging from a tree by his hair during battle.

name: Jethro
bio: Midianite priest and father-in-law of Moses. He gave his daughter Zipporah to Moses in marriage. After the Exodus, he visited Moses and advised him to delegate judicial authority to capable men, establishing a system of judges to lighten Moses' burden. In D&C 84, his priesthood lineage is traced.

name: Caleb
bio: One of the twelve spies sent by Moses to scout Canaan. Along with Joshua, he gave a faithful report encouraging Israel to take the land, while the other ten spies spread fear. For his faithfulness, he and Joshua were the only adults of their generation allowed to enter the promised land.

name: Balaam
bio: A Mesopotamian diviner hired by King Balak of Moab to curse Israel. God prevented him from cursing Israel and instead compelled him to bless them. His donkey famously saw an angel and spoke to Balaam. Despite his blessings, he later advised Balak to seduce Israel through Moabite women, leading to God's judgment.

name: Boaz
bio: A wealthy landowner in Bethlehem who showed kindness to Ruth the Moabite when she gleaned in his fields. He acted as kinsman-redeemer and married Ruth. He is an ancestor of King David and thus in the lineage of Jesus Christ.

name: Naomi
bio: An Israelite woman from Bethlehem who moved to Moab with her husband and two sons during a famine. After the deaths of her husband and sons, she returned to Bethlehem with her faithful daughter-in-law Ruth. She guided Ruth to Boaz, leading to their marriage and the birth of Obed, grandfather of King David.

name: Hosea
bio: A prophet in the northern kingdom of Israel during the 8th century BC. God commanded him to marry Gomer, an unfaithful woman, as a living parable of God's relationship with unfaithful Israel. His book emphasizes God's steadfast love and desire for Israel's repentance.

name: Amos
bio: A shepherd and fig farmer from Tekoa in Judah who was called to prophesy against the northern kingdom of Israel during the reign of Jeroboam II. He denounced social injustice, religious hypocrisy, and oppression of the poor. He declared, 'Let justice roll down like waters, and righteousness like an ever-flowing stream.'

name: Micah
bio: A prophet from Moresheth in Judah who was a contemporary of Isaiah. He prophesied judgment against Samaria and Jerusalem for injustice and idolatry. He foretold that the Messiah would be born in Bethlehem (Micah 5:2) and declared what God requires: 'to do justly, and to love mercy, and to walk humbly with thy God.'

name: Nahum
bio: A prophet from Elkosh who prophesied the destruction of Nineveh, the capital of Assyria. His prophecy, delivered about a century after Jonah's mission to Nineveh, declared that God's patience with the cruel Assyrian empire had ended.

name: Habakkuk
bio: A prophet who questioned God about why the wicked prosper and why God would use the Babylonians to punish Judah. God responded by assuring that judgment would come. Habakkuk's declaration, 'the just shall live by his faith,' became a key New Testament theme.

name: Zephaniah
bio: A prophet during the reign of King Josiah who warned of the coming 'day of the Lord' — a time of worldwide judgment. He prophesied against Judah's idolatry and the surrounding nations but concluded with a message of hope for a restored remnant.

name: Haggai
bio: A post-exilic prophet who urged the returned Jewish exiles to rebuild the temple in Jerusalem. He challenged them for living in paneled houses while the Lord's house lay in ruins. His encouragement led to the completion of the Second Temple.

name: Zechariah
bio: A post-exilic prophet who, along with Haggai, encouraged the rebuilding of the temple. His book contains apocalyptic visions and detailed Messianic prophecies, including the triumphant entry into Jerusalem on a donkey and the piercing of the Messiah.

name: Malachi
bio: The last Old Testament prophet, who prophesied around 430 BC. He rebuked the priests and people for corrupt worship and unfaithfulness. He prophesied the coming of Elijah before the 'great and dreadful day of the Lord' and the promise of tithing blessings. His words about Elijah are quoted in D&C 2 and 3 Nephi.

name: Ezra
bio: A priest and scribe who led a group of Jewish exiles from Babylon back to Jerusalem. He was instrumental in reestablishing the law of Moses among the returned exiles, reading the law publicly and leading reforms against intermarriage with surrounding peoples.

name: Nehemiah
bio: Cupbearer to King Artaxerxes of Persia who obtained permission to return to Jerusalem and rebuild its walls. Despite opposition from Sanballat and Tobiah, he completed the wall in 52 days. He also joined Ezra in religious reforms and served as governor of Judah.

name: Job
bio: A righteous and wealthy man from the land of Uz who was tested by Satan with God's permission. He lost his wealth, children, and health but refused to curse God. His friends argued he must have sinned, but Job maintained his innocence. God ultimately restored him to greater prosperity than before.

name: Jonah
bio: A prophet who was commanded to preach repentance to Nineveh but fled by ship instead. Swallowed by a great fish for three days, he repented and was delivered. He then preached to Nineveh, and the city repented. Jesus compared Jonah's time in the fish to his own death and resurrection.

name: Joel
bio: A prophet of Judah who used a devastating locust plague as a metaphor for the coming day of the Lord. He prophesied that God would pour out his Spirit on all flesh — a prophecy Peter cited as fulfilled at Pentecost.

name: Obadiah
bio: Author of the shortest book in the Old Testament. He prophesied judgment against Edom (descendants of Esau) for their pride and for their hostility toward Judah when Jerusalem was attacked.

name: Jeroboam
bio: First king of the northern kingdom of Israel after the split from Judah. He set up golden calves at Bethel and Dan to prevent his people from traveling to Jerusalem to worship, establishing a pattern of idolatry that persisted throughout the northern kingdom's history. He is repeatedly referenced as the standard of wickedness in Kings.

name: Rehoboam
bio: Son of Solomon who became king of Judah. His arrogant response to the people's plea for lighter burdens — threatening to increase them — caused ten tribes to break away under Jeroboam, splitting the united kingdom into Israel (north) and Judah (south).

name: Ahab
bio: King of the northern kingdom of Israel, married to the Phoenician princess Jezebel. He promoted Baal worship and clashed with the prophet Elijah. He is portrayed as one of the most wicked kings of Israel. He was killed by a random arrow in battle at Ramoth-gilead.

name: Jezebel
bio: Phoenician princess and wife of King Ahab of Israel. She promoted Baal worship, killed many prophets of the Lord, and orchestrated the judicial murder of Naboth to seize his vineyard. Elijah prophesied her violent death, which was fulfilled when she was thrown from a window and eaten by dogs.

name: Hezekiah
bio: Righteous king of Judah who implemented sweeping religious reforms, removing idols and high places. When threatened by the Assyrian army of Sennacherib, he prayed and Isaiah prophesied deliverance — an angel struck down 185,000 Assyrian soldiers. When told he would die, he prayed and God extended his life by fifteen years.

name: Josiah
bio: King of Judah who became king at age eight and led the last great reformation before the Babylonian exile. During temple repairs, the book of the law was discovered, prompting his sweeping reforms. He removed idolatry, restored Passover observance, and was praised as the most righteous king since David. He was killed in battle at Megiddo.

name: Zedekiah
bio: Last king of Judah, placed on the throne by Nebuchadnezzar. Despite Jeremiah's warnings, he rebelled against Babylon. He is mentioned in 1 Nephi as the reigning king when Lehi left Jerusalem. Jerusalem fell during his reign; he saw his sons killed and was blinded before being taken captive to Babylon.

name: Nebuchadnezzar
bio: King of Babylon who conquered Jerusalem, destroyed the temple, and carried the Jews into exile. He features prominently in the book of Daniel, where Daniel interpreted his dream of a great statue representing successive world kingdoms. He was humbled by God with a period of madness before acknowledging God's sovereignty.

name: Peter
bio: A fisherman from Galilee who became the chief apostle and leader of the early Christian church. He confessed Jesus as the Christ, witnessed the Transfiguration, denied Jesus three times before the crucifixion, and then became a bold witness of the Resurrection. He preached at Pentecost and opened the gospel to the Gentiles. In LDS theology, he appeared with James and John to restore the Melchizedek Priesthood.

name: Paul
bio: A Pharisee from Tarsus who persecuted Christians until his dramatic conversion on the road to Damascus, where the risen Jesus appeared to him. He became the greatest missionary of the early church, making multiple journeys throughout the Roman Empire and establishing churches among the Gentiles. He wrote thirteen or fourteen epistles that form a major portion of the New Testament.

name: John
bio: Son of Zebedee and brother of James, one of the original twelve apostles and part of Jesus' inner circle. He was present at the Transfiguration, in Gethsemane, and at the cross. He wrote the Gospel of John, three epistles, and the book of Revelation. LDS scripture teaches he was translated and continues to minister on the earth.

name: James
bio: Son of Zebedee and brother of John, one of the original twelve apostles and part of Jesus' inner circle along with Peter and John. He was present at the Transfiguration and in Gethsemane. He was the first apostle to be martyred, beheaded by Herod Agrippa I around AD 44. In LDS theology, he appeared with Peter and John to restore the Melchizedek Priesthood.

name: Mary
bio: A young woman of Nazareth, of the lineage of David, chosen to be the mother of Jesus Christ. The angel Gabriel announced she would conceive by the Holy Ghost. She bore Jesus in Bethlehem, was present at his first miracle in Cana, and stood at the foot of the cross. The Book of Mormon prophet Nephi saw her in vision as 'a virgin, most beautiful and fair.'

name: Joseph of Nazareth
bio: Husband of Mary and legal father of Jesus. A righteous carpenter descended from King David, he was warned in a dream by an angel not to put Mary away. He took his family to Egypt to escape Herod's slaughter of the innocents and later settled in Nazareth.

name: John the Baptist
bio: Son of Zacharias and Elisabeth, he was the forerunner of Jesus Christ. He preached repentance and baptized in the Jordan River, including baptizing Jesus himself. He was beheaded by Herod Antipas at the request of Salome. In LDS theology, he appeared as a resurrected being to Joseph Smith and Oliver Cowdery to restore the Aaronic Priesthood in 1829.

name: Andrew
bio: Brother of Simon Peter and one of the original twelve apostles. He was initially a disciple of John the Baptist before following Jesus. He brought his brother Peter to Jesus and was present at the feeding of the five thousand, where he identified the boy with five loaves and two fishes.

name: Philip
bio: One of the original twelve apostles, from Bethsaida in Galilee. He brought Nathanael to Jesus. At the feeding of the five thousand, Jesus tested Philip by asking how they could feed the crowd. He also brought certain Greeks to meet Jesus.

name: Bartholomew
bio: One of the original twelve apostles, often identified with Nathanael whom Philip brought to Jesus. When told about Jesus, he asked, 'Can any good thing come out of Nazareth?' Upon meeting Jesus, he was told, 'Behold, an Israelite indeed, in whom is no guile.'

name: Matthew
bio: A tax collector whom Jesus called to be one of the twelve apostles. He is traditionally credited as the author of the Gospel of Matthew, the first book of the New Testament. He hosted a feast for Jesus at his house, which drew criticism from the Pharisees.

name: Thomas
bio: One of the original twelve apostles, known for doubting Jesus' resurrection until he could see and touch the wounds. When he saw the risen Lord, he exclaimed, 'My Lord and my God!' He also showed courage when he said, 'Let us also go, that we may die with him,' when Jesus planned to return to Judea.

name: James, brother of Jesus
bio: A brother (or close relative) of Jesus who became a leader of the church in Jerusalem after the Resurrection. He presided over the Jerusalem Council in Acts 15 and is traditionally credited with writing the Epistle of James. He was known for his piety and was martyred around AD 62.

name: Judas Iscariot
bio: One of the original twelve apostles who betrayed Jesus for thirty pieces of silver by identifying him to the arresting party with a kiss in Gethsemane. He was the group's treasurer and had been stealing from the money bag. After the betrayal, he was filled with remorse and hanged himself.

name: Mary Magdalene
bio: A devoted follower of Jesus from whom he cast out seven devils. She was present at the crucifixion and was the first to see the risen Christ at the empty tomb on Easter morning. Jesus told her, 'Touch me not; for I am not yet ascended to my Father.'

name: Martha
bio: Sister of Mary and Lazarus, who lived in Bethany. She was rebuked by Jesus for being 'careful and troubled about many things' while Mary sat at his feet. She expressed profound faith when Lazarus died, declaring, 'I know, that even now, whatsoever thou wilt ask of God, God will give it thee.'

name: Lazarus
bio: Brother of Martha and Mary who lived in Bethany. He fell ill and died, and Jesus raised him from the dead after four days in the tomb — one of the most notable miracles in the Gospels. His resurrection led many to believe in Jesus, and the chief priests plotted to kill him because of this.

name: Stephen
bio: One of the first seven deacons chosen by the apostles to serve the early church. He was full of the Holy Ghost and performed great wonders. He delivered a powerful speech recounting Israel's history before the Sanhedrin and became the first Christian martyr when he was stoned to death. Saul (Paul) was present at his stoning.

name: Barnabas
bio: A Levite from Cyprus who was an early convert and important leader in the Christian church. He vouched for Paul after his conversion and accompanied Paul on his first missionary journey. Known as the 'son of consolation,' he later had a disagreement with Paul over John Mark and went on a separate mission to Cyprus.

name: Timothy
bio: A young convert from Lystra who became Paul's most trusted companion and protege. His mother Eunice was a Jewish believer and his grandmother Lois was also faithful. Paul wrote two epistles to him, advising him on church leadership and encouraging him to 'be thou an example of the believers.'

name: Luke
bio: A physician and companion of Paul, author of the Gospel of Luke and the Acts of the Apostles. He was a Gentile convert and the only non-Jewish author of a New Testament book. His Gospel emphasizes compassion, women, the poor, and the universal scope of salvation.

name: Mark
bio: Author of the Gospel of Mark, traditionally considered to have been based on Peter's preaching. He accompanied Paul and Barnabas on their first missionary journey but turned back, causing a rift between them. He later reconciled with Paul and was with him in Rome.

name: Herod the Great
bio: King of Judea under Rome who rebuilt the Temple in Jerusalem but was known for cruelty and paranoia. He ordered the slaughter of male children in Bethlehem after learning from the wise men that a new 'king of the Jews' had been born. He died shortly after Jesus' birth.

name: Herod Antipas
bio: Son of Herod the Great, tetrarch of Galilee during Jesus' ministry. He had John the Baptist beheaded at the request of his stepdaughter Salome, prompted by Herodias. Jesus was sent to him by Pilate for questioning before the crucifixion, but Herod mocked Jesus and returned him to Pilate.

name: Pontius Pilate
bio: Roman governor of Judea who presided over the trial of Jesus. Despite declaring he found no fault in Jesus, he yielded to the crowd's demands and sentenced him to crucifixion. He symbolically washed his hands to declare his innocence of Jesus' blood.

name: Nicodemus
bio: A Pharisee and member of the Sanhedrin who came to Jesus by night seeking to understand his teachings. Jesus taught him about being 'born again' of water and the Spirit. He later defended Jesus before the Sanhedrin and helped prepare Jesus' body for burial.

name: Elisabeth
bio: Wife of Zacharias and mother of John the Baptist. She was a descendant of Aaron and was barren until old age, when an angel announced she would bear a son. She was a relative of Mary, the mother of Jesus, and when Mary visited her, the babe in her womb leaped for joy.

name: Zacharias
bio: A priest of the course of Abijah and father of John the Baptist. While burning incense in the temple, the angel Gabriel appeared and announced that his wife Elisabeth would bear a son. Because he doubted, he was struck mute until the child's naming, when he prophesied and his speech returned.

name: Cornelius
bio: A Roman centurion in Caesarea described as devout and God-fearing. He received a vision directing him to send for Peter. Peter's vision of the sheet with unclean animals and his meeting with Cornelius marked the official opening of the gospel to the Gentiles. Cornelius and his household were baptized.

name: Simon the Zealot
bio: One of the original twelve apostles. Called 'the Zealot' (or 'Canaanite,' an Aramaic equivalent), suggesting he may have been associated with the Zealot movement before following Jesus. Little is recorded about his specific ministry.

name: Matthias
bio: Chosen by lot to replace Judas Iscariot as the twelfth apostle after the Ascension. He had been a follower of Jesus from the time of John's baptism. Little else is recorded about his subsequent ministry.

name: Gabriel
bio: An angel who serves as a messenger of God. He appeared to Zacharias to announce the birth of John the Baptist and to Mary to announce the birth of Jesus. He also appeared to Daniel in the Old Testament. In LDS theology, he is identified as the mortal Noah in his angelic role.

name: Michael
bio: An archangel mentioned in both the Old and New Testaments. He contended with the devil over the body of Moses and leads the heavenly hosts in the war in heaven described in Revelation. In LDS theology, Michael is identified as Adam in his pre-mortal and post-mortal roles.

name: Satan
bio: The chief of the fallen angels who rebelled against God in the pre-mortal existence. He tempted Adam and Eve in the Garden of Eden and continually seeks to lead humanity astray. He is portrayed as a being of deception and destruction across all volumes of scripture.

name: Lehi
bio: A prophet in Jerusalem around 600 BC who was warned by God to flee with his family before the Babylonian destruction. He led his family through the Arabian wilderness and across the ocean to the Americas. He had visions of the tree of life and of the future Messiah. He is a descendant of Joseph through Manasseh and the father of Nephi.

name: Sariah
bio: Wife of Lehi and mother of Laman, Lemuel, Sam, Nephi, Jacob, and Joseph. She murmured against Lehi when their sons were in danger retrieving the brass plates from Laban but was comforted when they returned safely. She endured the hardships of the wilderness journey and ocean voyage to the promised land. She is one of only three non-biblical women named in the Book of Mormon.

name: Nephi
bio: Fourth son of Lehi and Sariah, and the principal author and narrator of 1 Nephi and 2 Nephi. Known for his great faith, he obtained the brass plates from Laban, built a ship to cross the ocean, and became the first leader of the Nephite people. He saw sweeping visions of Christ and the future. He declared, 'I will go and do the things which the Lord hath commanded.'

name: Laman
bio: Eldest son of Lehi and Sariah. He repeatedly rebelled against his father and brother Nephi, refusing to believe in the prophetic calling. He and Lemuel frequently murmured and even physically abused Nephi. After arriving in the promised land, his descendants became known as the Lamanites.

name: Lemuel
bio: Second son of Lehi and Sariah. He sided with his eldest brother Laman in rebelling against Lehi and Nephi. He participated in the murmuring and opposition during the wilderness journey and ocean crossing. He joined Laman in separating from Nephi after Lehi's death.

name: Sam
bio: Third son of Lehi and Sariah. He believed Nephi's words and supported him faithfully. Though he did not play a prominent leadership role, his loyalty was recognized and Lehi promised that his seed would be blessed with Nephi's seed.

name: Jacob
bio: Fifth son of Lehi and Sariah, born in the wilderness. He was ordained a priest and teacher by Nephi. He authored the Book of Jacob in the Book of Mormon and delivered a powerful sermon against pride, materialism, and unchastity. He recorded the allegory of the olive tree from the prophet Zenos.

name: Joseph
bio: Youngest son of Lehi and Sariah, born in the wilderness during the family's journey. Lehi gave him a special blessing referencing the prophecies of Joseph of Egypt about a future seer named Joseph (Joseph Smith) who would come through his lineage.

name: Zoram
bio: Servant of Laban in Jerusalem who accompanied Nephi when Nephi obtained the brass plates. He initially thought Nephi was Laban and went with him willingly. When his identity was discovered, he was persuaded to join Lehi's family and swore an oath. His descendants became known as the Zoramites.

name: Laban
bio: A powerful man in Jerusalem who possessed the brass plates (a record of the Jews containing the scriptures). He refused to give them to Nephi's brothers and tried to kill them and steal their property. He was slain by Nephi, who was commanded by the Spirit to do so in order to obtain the plates.

name: Ishmael
bio: A man in Jerusalem whose family joined Lehi's family in the wilderness. His daughters married Lehi's sons, providing wives for the journey. He died and was buried in a place called Nahom during the wilderness trek. His sons and daughters were divided in their loyalties between Nephi and Laman.

name: Enos
bio: Son of Jacob (son of Lehi) and author of the Book of Enos. He is known for his prolonged, wrestle-in-prayer experience while hunting, during which he received forgiveness of his sins and then prayed for the Nephites and Lamanites. He kept the records and passed them to his son Jarom.

name: Jarom
bio: Son of Enos and author of the Book of Jarom. He briefly recorded that the Nephites prospered by keeping the law of Moses and looking forward to the Messiah. He passed the records to his son Omni.

name: Omni
bio: Son of Jarom and author of the first portion of the Book of Omni. He described himself as a wicked man who had not kept the commandments but fulfilled his duty to keep the record. He passed the plates to his son Amaron.

name: King Benjamin
bio: A righteous Nephite king who fought off Lamanite invaders and established peace. He delivered one of the most important sermons in the Book of Mormon from a tower in Zarahemla, teaching about the atonement of Christ and service. His people experienced a mighty change of heart and entered into a covenant with God. He passed the kingdom to his son Mosiah.

name: Mosiah I
bio: A Nephite king who led a group of righteous Nephites out of the land of Nephi to the land of Zarahemla, where they joined with the people of Zarahemla (Mulekites). He became king over both peoples and was the father of King Benjamin.

name: Mosiah II
bio: Son of King Benjamin who succeeded him as king of the Nephites. He was a seer who translated the Jaredite record using interpreters. Concerned about future contentions over the throne, he established a system of judges to replace the monarchy. He also sent Ammon's expedition to find the people of Zeniff.

name: Alma the Elder
bio: A priest in the court of King Noah who believed the prophet Abinadi's words and secretly recorded them. He fled into the wilderness, baptized believers at the Waters of Mormon, and established the church among the Nephites. He served as the first high priest over the church and was the father of Alma the Younger.

name: Alma the Younger
bio: Son of Alma the Elder who initially rebelled against the church and sought to destroy it. He was visited by an angel, fell unconscious for three days, and experienced a dramatic conversion. He became the first chief judge of the Nephites and later resigned to preach full-time. He is one of the most prominent figures in the Book of Mormon, with extensive sermons and missionary accounts.

name: Abinadi
bio: A prophet among the Nephites who was sent to warn King Noah and his wicked priests to repent. He boldly testified of Jesus Christ and expounded the law of Moses and the coming atonement. He was burned to death for refusing to deny his testimony, becoming the Book of Mormon's most prominent martyr. His teachings converted Alma the Elder.

name: King Noah
bio: A wicked Nephite king who taxed his people heavily, surrounded himself with corrupt priests, built extravagant buildings, and promoted wickedness. He had the prophet Abinadi burned to death. He was eventually overthrown by his own people and burned to death himself, as Abinadi had prophesied.

name: Ammon
bio: Son of King Mosiah who, after his conversion with Alma the Younger, became a missionary to the Lamanites. He served King Lamoni, defending his flocks by cutting off the arms of attackers. His service and testimony led to the conversion of King Lamoni and many Lamanites. He is one of the most celebrated missionaries in the Book of Mormon.

name: Aaron
bio: Son of King Mosiah and brother of Ammon who served as a missionary to the Lamanites. He was imprisoned in Middoni before being freed. He taught King Lamoni's father (the over-king of the Lamanites), leading to his dramatic conversion. His missionary labors helped establish the Anti-Nephi-Lehies.

name: Amulek
bio: A wealthy Nephite in Ammonihah who was visited by an angel and told to receive Alma into his home. He became Alma's missionary companion and together they preached in Ammonihah. He witnessed the burning of believing women and children by the wicked Ammonihahites and debated the lawyer Zeezrom, who later converted.

name: Captain Moroni
bio: Chief captain of the Nephite armies, appointed at age twenty-five. He was a brilliant military strategist who fortified Nephite cities and led them through years of war against the Lamanites and the dissenter Amalickiah. He created the 'title of liberty' to rally his people. Mormon wrote that if all men had been like Moroni, the powers of hell would have been shaken forever.

name: Helaman
bio: Eldest son of Alma the Younger who received the sacred records and led the two thousand stripling warriors — young Lamanite converts who fought with miraculous protection. He served as a military leader and later as high priest. None of his young warriors were killed in battle, which he attributed to the faith taught them by their mothers.

name: Helaman
bio: Son of Helaman (son of Alma) and chief judge of the Nephites. He named his sons Nephi and Lehi to remind them of their righteous ancestors. His record covers a period of Nephite prosperity followed by wickedness and Gadianton robber infiltration.

name: Nephi
bio: Son of Helaman (son of Helaman) who served as chief judge before resigning to preach full-time with his brother Lehi. He performed mighty miracles, including prophetically identifying the murderer of the chief judge. He was given the sealing power and called down a famine to humble the people. He and his brother Lehi were once surrounded by fire while in prison.

name: Samuel the Lamanite
bio: A Lamanite prophet who stood on the walls of Zarahemla and prophesied to the Nephites around 6 BC. He prophesied signs of Christ's birth (a day, a night, and a day without darkness; a new star) and signs of his death (three days of darkness, earthquakes). He could not be hit by arrows or stones and escaped over the wall when the people tried to seize him.

name: Mormon
bio: A Nephite prophet, historian, and military leader who compiled and abridged the records of his people into the Book of Mormon. He took command of the Nephite armies at age fifteen and witnessed the final destruction of the Nephite nation. He was both a spiritual and military leader during the darkest period of Nephite history. The Book of Mormon is named after him.

name: Moroni
bio: Son of Mormon and the last Nephite prophet. After his father's death, he completed the Book of Mormon record, abridged the Jaredite record (Book of Ether), and wrote the Book of Moroni. He wandered alone for decades, writing his final testimonies. He buried the plates in the Hill Cumorah. As a resurrected being, he appeared to Joseph Smith in 1823 and directed him to the plates.

name: Amalickiah
bio: A Nephite dissenter who sought to become king. When Captain Moroni raised the title of liberty against him, he fled to the Lamanites with a small group of followers. Through treachery and murder, he became king of the Lamanites and launched a massive war against the Nephites. He was killed by Teancum with a javelin while he slept.

name: Ammoron
bio: Brother of Amalickiah who became king of the Lamanites after Amalickiah's death. He continued the war against the Nephites and exchanged sharp epistles with Captain Moroni regarding prisoner exchanges. He was eventually killed by Teancum, who also died in the attempt.

name: Teancum
bio: A brave Nephite military commander during the wars with the Lamanites. He personally killed Amalickiah by sneaking into his tent and putting a javelin through his heart. He later attempted the same tactic against Ammoron but was discovered and killed, though he succeeded in killing Ammoron as well.

name: Pahoran
bio: Chief judge of the Nephites during the war chapters. He received a sharp letter from Captain Moroni accusing him of neglecting the armies. Pahoran's gracious response — explaining he had been driven from the judgment seat by king-men — is one of the Book of Mormon's notable exchanges. He joined forces with Moroni to recapture Zarahemla.

name: Zeniff
bio: A Nephite who led a group back to the land of Nephi to reclaim it. He made a treaty with the Lamanite king but was eventually attacked. Though zealous, his overeagerness led his people into a vulnerable situation. He was the father of King Noah and grandfather of Limhi.

name: Limhi
bio: Son of King Noah and grandson of Zeniff. He became king after his father's death and ruled his people who were in bondage to the Lamanites. He was a righteous king who eventually led his people to escape Lamanite oppression and join the Nephites in Zarahemla.

name: Lamoni
bio: A Lamanite king converted by the missionary Ammon. When Ammon defended his flocks, Lamoni was so impressed that he listened to Ammon's teachings. He fell unconscious for two days during his conversion experience. He allowed Ammon to preach throughout his lands and became a devoted believer.

name: Anti-Nephi-Lehi
bio: Brother of King Lamoni who became king of the converted Lamanites. Under his leadership, the converts buried their weapons of war and refused to fight, preferring to die rather than risk sinning again. Many of them were killed by attacking Lamanites, and the attackers were so moved that more Lamanites converted.

name: Abish
bio: A Lamanite servant woman in King Lamoni's court who had been converted years earlier through a vision her father had. When King Lamoni, his wife, Ammon, and others fell to the earth during a spiritual experience, Abish recognized what was happening and ran to gather the people, hoping they would believe. She is one of only three non-biblical women named in the Book of Mormon.

name: Zeezrom
bio: A skilled lawyer in Ammonihah who initially tried to trap Alma and Amulek with cunning questions and bribery. He was confounded by Amulek and struck with a burning fever from guilt. He repented, was healed by Alma, and became a dedicated missionary and defender of the faith.

name: Korihor
bio: An anti-Christ who preached against the prophets, the coming of Christ, and the atonement in the land of Zarahemla. He demanded a sign from Alma and was struck dumb. He then confessed that the devil had appeared to him as an angel and taught him what to say. He was eventually trampled to death among the Zoramites.

name: Corianton
bio: Youngest son of Alma the Younger who abandoned his mission to the Zoramites to pursue the harlot Isabel. His father addressed three chapters of counsel to him (Alma 39–42), explaining the seriousness of sexual sin and the plan of mercy and justice. He eventually repented and returned to missionary service.

name: Shiblon
bio: Second son of Alma the Younger, praised by his father for his faithfulness and steadiness. He served as a missionary and briefly held the sacred records between Helaman's death and passing them to Helaman's son. Alma compared him favorably to his troubled brother Corianton.

name: Gidgiddoni
bio: Chief judge and military commander of the Nephites who led them to a decisive victory over the Gadianton robbers. He was described as a great prophet as well as a military leader. He gathered the Nephites together and prepared them with provisions and armor to defeat the robbers.

name: Nephi
bio: A prophet at the time of Christ's appearance to the Nephites. He prayed for his people and was visited by Jesus, who called him first among the twelve Nephite disciples. He baptized the people and was given power to perform great miracles.

name: Isabel
bio: A harlot in the land of Siron who enticed Corianton, son of Alma the Younger, away from his missionary duties among the Zoramites. She is one of only three non-biblical women named in the Book of Mormon. Alma rebuked his son severely for going after her.

name: Giddianhi
bio: Leader of the Gadianton robbers who wrote a threatening letter to Lachoneus, the Nephite governor, demanding surrender. The letter is notable for its flattery and audacity. He led the robber armies against the gathered Nephites and was killed in battle.

name: Lachoneus
bio: Chief judge and governor of the Nephites who received the threatening epistle from Giddianhi. He wisely gathered the entire Nephite nation into one defensible area with provisions, rather than marching out to fight the Gadianton robbers. This strategy led to the robbers' defeat.

name: Brother of Jared
bio: A man of great faith who led the Jaredites from the Tower of Babel to the Americas. He prepared barges for the ocean crossing and brought sixteen stones to the Lord, asking him to touch them to provide light. The Lord touched the stones, and because of his exceptional faith, the brother of Jared saw the pre-mortal body of Christ. Moroni called his faith the greatest ever recorded.

name: Jared
bio: Brother of the 'brother of Jared' who led the Jaredite colony from the Tower of Babel to the Americas. He asked his brother to pray that their language not be confounded and that they be led to a promised land. He became a leader among the Jaredites in their new homeland.

name: Ether
bio: The last Jaredite prophet whose record was found by the people of Limhi and later abridged by Moroni into the Book of Ether. He prophesied to King Coriantumr and the Jaredites but was rejected. He hid in a cavity of a rock and witnessed the final destruction of the Jaredite civilization.

name: Coriantumr
bio: The last Jaredite king who fought in the final devastating civil war that destroyed the Jaredite nation. The prophet Ether warned him that if he did not repent, his entire civilization would be destroyed and he alone would survive. He fought Shiz until only the two of them remained; Coriantumr killed Shiz and was later found by the people of Zarahemla.

name: Shiz
bio: A Jaredite military leader who fought against Coriantumr in the final Jaredite wars. He pursued Coriantumr relentlessly, and the two sides destroyed virtually the entire Jaredite civilization in their conflict. He was the last opponent Coriantumr faced and was killed by him.

name: Ammon
bio: A strong Nephite, descendant of Zarahemla, who led an expedition from Zarahemla to find the people of Zeniff. He discovered King Limhi's people in bondage to the Lamanites. He is distinct from Ammon the son of Mosiah.

name: Amaleki
bio: Son of Abinadom and the final writer on the small plates of Nephi. He recorded the reign of King Benjamin and encouraged all people to come unto Christ. He had no seed and entrusted the plates to King Benjamin.

name: Abinadom
bio: A Nephite record keeper and warrior, son of Chemish. He described himself as having seen much war between the Nephites and Lamanites. He passed the records to his son Amaleki.

name: Chemish
bio: Brother of Amaron who briefly contributed to the Book of Omni. He recorded that he saw his brother write in the book and passed the record to his son Abinadom.

name: Amaron
bio: Son of Omni who briefly kept the Nephite records. He recorded that the more wicked Nephites were destroyed according to the Lord's word. He gave the plates to his brother Chemish.

name: Antipus
bio: A Nephite military commander who, with Helaman and the stripling warriors, devised a strategy to lure Lamanite armies out of fortified cities. His forces were weakened from battle but joined the young warriors in defeating the pursuing Lamanites.

name: Gadianton
bio: The founder of the Gadianton robbers, a secret combination that plagued Nephite society for centuries. He was expert in 'many words and also in his craft' to carry on secret work. His band swore secret oaths and covenants patterned after those of Cain and the Jaredite secret combinations.

name: Kishkumen
bio: An assassin who murdered the chief judge Pahoran (son of Pahoran) and later attempted to murder Helaman (son of Helaman). He was killed by a servant of Helaman who intercepted him. He was an early leader of the band that Gadianton later took over.

name: Hagoth
bio: A Nephite shipbuilder described as 'an exceedingly curious man.' He built a large ship and sailed northward from the narrow neck of land with many Nephites and provisions. He built other ships as well. Polynesian Latter-day Saints have traditionally connected Hagoth with the ancestors of Pacific Island peoples.

name: Joseph Smith
bio: Founder and first prophet of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints. At age fourteen, he experienced the First Vision in which God the Father and Jesus Christ appeared to him. He translated the Book of Mormon from gold plates delivered by the angel Moroni, organized the Church in 1830, received numerous revelations recorded in the Doctrine and Covenants, and was martyred in Carthage, Illinois in 1844.

name: Oliver Cowdery
bio: Served as principal scribe during the translation of the Book of Mormon and was one of the Three Witnesses who saw the gold plates and the angel Moroni. He was present when John the Baptist restored the Aaronic Priesthood and when Peter, James, and John restored the Melchizedek Priesthood. He is mentioned in more D&C sections than any person besides Joseph Smith.

name: Emma Smith
bio: Wife of Joseph Smith and the 'elect lady' addressed in D&C 25. She served as scribe during part of the Book of Mormon translation, compiled the first LDS hymnal, and was the first president of the Relief Society. She endured severe trials including the loss of children and her husband's martyrdom.

name: Sidney Rigdon
bio: A former Campbellite preacher who converted to the Church in 1830 and became Joseph Smith's counselor in the First Presidency. He shared the vision of the three degrees of glory recorded in D&C 76. He served as a spokesman and scribe. After Joseph Smith's death, he was rejected as leader in favor of Brigham Young.

name: Hyrum Smith
bio: Older brother of Joseph Smith and Assistant President of the Church, later ordained as Church Patriarch. He was a steadfast supporter of Joseph throughout the Restoration. He was martyred alongside Joseph at Carthage Jail in June 1844. D&C 124:15 describes him as having 'the integrity of his heart.'

name: Brigham Young
bio: Second president of The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints who led the Saints westward to the Salt Lake Valley after Joseph Smith's martyrdom. He served as president of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and organized the massive pioneer migration. He is addressed in several D&C sections as a member of the Twelve.

name: Martin Harris
bio: One of the Three Witnesses of the Book of Mormon who saw the gold plates and the angel Moroni. He financed the first printing of the Book of Mormon. He was entrusted with 116 pages of the manuscript which his wife lost, resulting in the Lord's rebuke in D&C 3 and 10. He eventually returned to the Church and died in Utah.

name: David Whitmer
bio: One of the Three Witnesses of the Book of Mormon. The translation was completed at his family's farm in Fayette, New York. He saw the gold plates and the angel Moroni. Though he was excommunicated in 1838, he maintained his testimony of the Book of Mormon until his death.

name: John Whitmer
bio: One of the Eight Witnesses of the Book of Mormon and brother of David Whitmer. He served as the Church's first historian, as commanded in D&C 47. He was excommunicated in 1838 but never denied his testimony of the Book of Mormon.

name: Peter Whitmer Jr.
bio: One of the Eight Witnesses of the Book of Mormon and brother of David and John Whitmer. The Church was organized at his parents' home in Fayette, New York on April 6, 1830. He served a mission to the Lamanites (Native Americans) and died in 1836.

name: Joseph Smith Sr.
bio: Father of the Prophet Joseph Smith and one of the Eight Witnesses of the Book of Mormon. He was the first Patriarch of the Church. He supported his son's prophetic calling from the earliest days and served faithfully until his death in 1840.

name: Lucy Mack Smith
bio: Mother of the Prophet Joseph Smith. She wrote a detailed history of her son and the early Church. She was a woman of great faith who endured the loss of several children, including Joseph and Hyrum at Carthage. Her memoir is an important primary source for early Church history.

name: Parley P. Pratt
bio: An early apostle and prolific writer who authored 'A Voice of Warning' and other missionary tracts. He was among the first missionaries to the Lamanites and played a key role in converting Sidney Rigdon. He served in the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and was martyred in Arkansas in 1857.

name: Orson Pratt
bio: An early apostle, theologian, and mathematician who was baptized at age nineteen by his brother Parley P. Pratt. He served in the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and was known for his intellectual defense of Church doctrines. He prepared the first edition of the Book of Mormon divided into verses.

name: Orson Hyde
bio: An early apostle who, in 1841, traveled to Jerusalem and offered a dedicatory prayer on the Mount of Olives for the gathering of the Jews. He served in the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and is addressed in several D&C sections.

name: John Taylor
bio: Third president of the Church who was present at Carthage Jail when Joseph and Hyrum Smith were killed. He was severely wounded but survived. He later served as president and declared, 'Joseph Smith, the Prophet and Seer of the Lord, has done more, save Jesus only, for the salvation of men in this world, than any other man that ever lived in it' (D&C 135).

name: Wilford Woodruff
bio: Fourth president of the Church who issued the 1890 Manifesto ending the practice of plural marriage. He was a prolific journal keeper whose diaries are invaluable historical records. He served as an apostle for decades before becoming Church president. Official Declaration 1 in the D&C bears his name.

name: Thomas B. Marsh
bio: First president of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles. He received a personal revelation (D&C 31) and was told to 'be patient in afflictions.' He later apostatized over a dispute but returned to the Church in 1857 and bore testimony of its truthfulness.

name: Edward Partridge
bio: First bishop of the Church, called by revelation in D&C 41. He was a hatter from Painesville, Ohio. He was tarred and feathered in Missouri for his faith. He served faithfully until his death in 1840.

name: Newel K. Whitney
bio: Second Presiding Bishop of the Church. He owned a store in Kirtland, Ohio, where Joseph Smith lived and received many revelations. He is mentioned in several D&C sections and served faithfully as bishop during challenging periods of Church history.

name: Frederick G. Williams
bio: A counselor to Joseph Smith in the First Presidency and one of the early leaders of the Church. He served as a scribe and physician. He is mentioned in several D&C sections and helped compile the 1835 edition of the Doctrine and Covenants.

name: W. W. Phelps
bio: An early convert who served as printer, editor, and hymn writer for the Church. He apostatized and testified against Joseph Smith in Missouri but later repented and was forgiven. Joseph Smith's famous letter to him, 'Come on, dear brother, since the war is past,' is preserved. He wrote the hymn 'The Spirit of God.'

name: Ezra Thayre
bio: An early Church member who received a personal revelation (D&C 33) calling him to preach the gospel. He was called on a mission but struggled with obedience. He is one of many early members specifically addressed by name in the Doctrine and Covenants.

name: Orson Spencer
bio: An educated convert who gave up a career as a Baptist minister to join the Church. He became chancellor of the University of Deseret (now University of Utah) and served a mission to England.

name: Lyman Wight
bio: An early apostle and member of Zion's Camp. He is mentioned in D&C 124 as one called to the Quorum of the Twelve. After Joseph Smith's death, he led a group of Saints to Texas rather than follow Brigham Young.

name: Heber C. Kimball
bio: A member of the original Quorum of the Twelve Apostles and later first counselor in the First Presidency under Brigham Young. He led the first mission to England in 1837, which was remarkably successful. He was known for his strong faith and practical wisdom.

name: Willard Richards
bio: An apostle and Church historian who was present at Carthage Jail when Joseph and Hyrum Smith were killed. He was the only one of the four men present who was not wounded, fulfilling Joseph Smith's earlier prophecy. He later served as second counselor to Brigham Young.

name: Melchizedek
bio: King of Salem and priest of the most high God who blessed Abraham after his victory over the kings. He received tithes from Abraham. The higher priesthood bears his name (Melchizedek Priesthood) because his name was used to avoid the frequent repetition of the name of God. He is described in Alma 13 as a great high priest who established peace.

name: Shem
bio: Son of Noah who survived the flood and became the ancestor of the Semitic peoples. He was blessed by Noah and his tent was a place of righteousness. In LDS tradition, some identify him with Melchizedek, though this is not canonized doctrine.

name: Ham
bio: Son of Noah who saw his father's nakedness after the flood, for which his son Canaan was cursed. He became the ancestor of various peoples in Africa and the Near East. He is mentioned in the Pearl of Great Price in connection with his wife Egyptus.

name: Japheth
bio: Son of Noah who survived the flood. He and his brother Shem covered their father's nakedness by walking backward with a garment. He became the ancestor of various Indo-European peoples.

name: Levi
bio: Third son of Jacob and Leah, ancestor of the priestly tribe of Levi. He and Simeon attacked the city of Shechem in retaliation for the defilement of their sister Dinah. His descendants, the Levites, were consecrated for temple service, and Aaron's branch held the priesthood.

name: Reuben
bio: Firstborn son of Jacob and Leah. He lost his birthright for an offense against his father. He tried to save Joseph from being sold by suggesting they put him in a pit rather than kill him. His tribe settled east of the Jordan River.

name: Benjamin
bio: Youngest son of Jacob and Rachel, who died giving birth to him near Bethlehem. Jacob was especially protective of him after Joseph's supposed death. The tribe of Benjamin later produced King Saul and the apostle Paul.

name: Ephraim
bio: Younger son of Joseph and Asenath who received Jacob's primary blessing despite being the younger brother. His tribe became one of the most prominent in the northern kingdom of Israel. In LDS theology, the gathering of Israel is primarily through the tribe of Ephraim, and most Latter-day Saints are declared to be of Ephraim in patriarchal blessings.

name: Manasseh
bio: Elder son of Joseph and Asenath. Though the firstborn, he received the secondary blessing from Jacob while Ephraim received the greater. The Book of Mormon identifies Lehi's family as descendants of Manasseh (Alma 10:3), connecting the Nephites to this tribe.

name: Rahab
bio: A harlot in Jericho who hid the Israelite spies and helped them escape by lowering them from her window with a scarlet cord. In exchange, her family was spared when Jericho was destroyed. She is mentioned in the genealogy of Jesus Christ and praised in the New Testament for her faith.

name: Tamar
bio: Daughter-in-law of Judah who disguised herself as a prostitute to secure offspring through Judah after he failed to provide his third son as her husband. She bore twins Perez and Zerah. She appears in the genealogy of Jesus Christ in Matthew 1.

name: Mordecai
bio: A Jewish leader in the Persian court and guardian of his cousin Esther. He uncovered a plot to assassinate the king and later refused to bow to the wicked Haman. His actions, combined with Esther's courage, led to the salvation of the Jewish people from Haman's genocide plot.

name: Shadrach
bio: One of Daniel's three companions who were thrown into a fiery furnace by King Nebuchadnezzar for refusing to worship a golden image. They survived unharmed, and a fourth figure 'like the Son of God' was seen walking with them in the fire. His Hebrew name was Hananiah.

name: Meshach
bio: One of Daniel's three companions cast into the fiery furnace for refusing to worship Nebuchadnezzar's golden image. He emerged unharmed along with Shadrach and Abednego. His Hebrew name was Mishael.

name: Abednego
bio: One of Daniel's three companions who survived the fiery furnace when thrown in for refusing to worship Nebuchadnezzar's golden image. His Hebrew name was Azariah.

name: Goliath
bio: A Philistine giant from Gath, over nine feet tall, who challenged the armies of Israel to single combat. The young shepherd David accepted the challenge and killed him with a single stone from a sling, then beheaded him with Goliath's own sword.

name: Naaman
bio: A commander of the Syrian army who had leprosy. The Israelite prophet Elisha told him to wash seven times in the Jordan River to be healed. After initial reluctance (he expected something more dramatic), he obeyed and was healed. Jesus referenced his healing to illustrate that prophets are not always honored among their own people.

name: Jephthah
bio: A judge of Israel, the son of Gilead by a prostitute, who was initially rejected by his half-brothers. He was called back to lead Israel against the Ammonites. He made a rash vow to sacrifice whatever came out of his house first if victorious — tragically, his daughter came out to greet him.

name: Mary of Bethany
bio: Sister of Martha and Lazarus who lived in Bethany. She sat at Jesus' feet to learn while Martha served, prompting Jesus to say she had chosen 'the good part.' She anointed Jesus' feet with expensive ointment and wiped them with her hair before his final Passover.

name: Priscilla
bio: Wife of Aquila and an early Christian leader who, with her husband, was a tentmaker and coworker of Paul. They helped instruct Apollos more accurately in the faith. Paul praised them as those who 'laid down their own necks' for his life.

name: Aquila
bio: A Jewish tentmaker from Pontus and husband of Priscilla. He and his wife were expelled from Rome under Claudius and settled in Corinth, where they met and worked with Paul. They traveled with Paul and hosted a church in their home.

name: Apollos
bio: An eloquent Alexandrian Jew who was mighty in the scriptures. He preached boldly in Ephesus but knew only the baptism of John until Priscilla and Aquila instructed him more fully. He became a powerful preacher in Corinth, and Paul mentioned him alongside himself as fellow workers.

name: Philemon
bio: A wealthy Christian in Colossae to whom Paul wrote a personal letter asking him to receive back his runaway slave Onesimus — not as a slave but as a brother in Christ. Paul's epistle to Philemon is the shortest of his letters.

name: Titus
bio: A Greek convert who was a trusted companion of Paul. Paul sent him to Corinth to help resolve problems and later left him in Crete to organize churches. Paul's epistle to Titus provides instructions on church leadership.

name: Dorcas
bio: A disciple in Joppa known for her charitable works. When she died, Peter was summoned, and he raised her from the dead, causing many to believe in the Lord. She is one of the few women in the New Testament explicitly called a disciple.

name: Lydia
bio: A seller of purple cloth from Thyatira who was Paul's first convert in Europe, in the city of Philippi. She was baptized with her household and insisted Paul and Silas stay at her home, which became a meeting place for the church.

name: Caiaphas
bio: High priest during the ministry and trial of Jesus. He prophesied (unknowingly) that it was expedient for one man to die for the people. He presided over the trials of Jesus and later Peter and John. He was son-in-law of Annas.

name: Barabbas
bio: A prisoner held by the Romans at the time of Jesus' trial. Pilate offered the crowd a choice between releasing Jesus or Barabbas, and the crowd demanded Barabbas be freed. He was described as a robber and insurrectionist who had committed murder.

name: Joseph of Arimathea
bio: A wealthy member of the Jewish council who was a secret disciple of Jesus. After the crucifixion, he asked Pilate for Jesus' body and provided his own new tomb for the burial. He is mentioned in all four Gospels.

name: Silas
bio: A leader in the Jerusalem church who accompanied Paul on his second missionary journey. He and Paul were imprisoned in Philippi, where an earthquake freed them. He is also associated with Peter and may have assisted in writing 1 Peter.

name: Jared
bio: Father of Enoch in the line from Adam to Noah. He lived 962 years according to Genesis. He is mentioned in the genealogies of Genesis 5 and 1 Chronicles 1, as well as in the Book of Moses in the Pearl of Great Price.

name: Lamech
bio: Son of Methuselah and father of Noah. He lived 777 years. He named his son Noah, saying, 'This same shall comfort us concerning our work and toil of our hands.' He is in the direct line from Adam to Noah.

name: Orihah
bio: Son of Jared and the first king of the Jaredites. He was anointed king despite his father and the brother of Jared having refused the monarchy. He reigned righteously and lived to an old age.

name: Akish
bio: A wicked Jaredite who formed a secret combination to obtain power. He conspired to kill King Omer in order to marry Jared's daughter. He became king but his secret combinations led to devastating civil wars among the Jaredites.

name: Riplakish
bio: A wicked Jaredite king who did not do that which was right. He taxed the people heavily, built many prisons, and had many wives and concubines. He was eventually overthrown and killed by his own people.

name: Lib
bio: A Jaredite king described as a righteous ruler under whom the Jaredites prospered greatly. The land was developed, and the people grew exceedingly rich during his reign.

name: Morianton
bio: A Jaredite king, descendant of Riplakish, who gathered an army of outcasts and overthrew the ruling king. He established himself as king and dealt justly with the people, though he was personally immoral.

name: Amulon
bio: Leader of the wicked priests of King Noah who fled into the wilderness after Noah's death. He and the other priests kidnapped Lamanite daughters as wives. He later became a teacher over Alma's people in bondage and persecuted them for praying.

name: Gideon
bio: A strong Nephite man who opposed King Noah and tried to slay him. He was a military leader who later served as a teacher and advisor in the church. He was killed by the anti-Christ Nehor. A city and valley were named after him.

name: Nehor
bio: An anti-Christ in the Book of Mormon who taught that all mankind would be saved regardless of their actions, promoted priestcraft, and killed Gideon when confronted. He was brought before Alma the Younger, convicted of priestcraft and murder, and executed. His teachings persisted through his followers.

name: Amlici
bio: A cunning Nephite who sought to become king after the transition to the reign of judges. He gathered followers and led a rebellion. Though defeated by the Nephites, he allied with the Lamanites and attacked again before being killed by Alma the Younger in personal combat.

name: Aminadi
bio: An ancestor of Amulek who was notable for interpreting writing on the wall of the temple that was written by the finger of God. He was a descendant of Nephi, son of Lehi, and of Joseph who was sold into Egypt.

name: Aminadab
bio: A Nephite dissenter living among the Lamanites who witnessed Nephi (son of Helaman) and his brother Lehi surrounded by fire while in prison. He recognized the divine manifestation and told the other Lamanites to repent, leading to the conversion of about 300 people.

name: Lehi
bio: Son of Helaman (son of Helaman) and brother of Nephi. He was named after the original Lehi to inspire him to remember his ancestor's goodness. He served as a missionary with his brother Nephi and was imprisoned. While in prison, both brothers were surrounded by fire and the prison walls fell.

name: Ammaron
bio: Brother of Amos, a Nephite record keeper who hid the sacred records in the hill Shim. He instructed the young Mormon, at age ten, to take the records when he was old enough and continue the record of his people.

name: Zarahemla
bio: Descendant of Mulek (son of King Zedekiah) and leader of the people of Zarahemla (Mulekites) when they were discovered by Mosiah I. He gave a genealogy of his fathers. His people had come from Jerusalem at the time of the Babylonian captivity but had lost their language and records. The major Nephite capital city was named after him.

name: Mulek
bio: A son of King Zedekiah of Judah who escaped the Babylonian destruction of Jerusalem and was led to the Americas. His descendants, the Mulekites (people of Zarahemla), were discovered by Mosiah I. Though Mulek himself is not mentioned as a speaking character, his lineage is significant in Book of Mormon geography and history.

name: Omner
bio: Son of King Mosiah and one of the four missionary sons who served among the Lamanites. He was initially a rebel with Alma the Younger before their collective conversion by an angel. He spent fourteen years as a missionary among the Lamanites.

name: Himni
bio: Son of King Mosiah and one of the four missionary sons who preached to the Lamanites. Like his brothers, he had rebelled against the church before being converted by an angelic visitation. He joined the fourteen-year mission to the Lamanites.

name: Samuel Smith
bio: Younger brother of Joseph Smith and one of the Eight Witnesses of the Book of Mormon. He served one of the first missions for the Church, distributing copies of the Book of Mormon. He died shortly after the martyrdom of Joseph and Hyrum.

name: Lyman E. Johnson
bio: One of the original members of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles, called in 1835. He was among the youngest of the original Twelve. He later left the Church and was excommunicated in 1838.

name: William McLellin
bio: One of the original members of the Quorum of the Twelve Apostles. He received several revelations through Joseph Smith (D&C 66, 68). He later apostatized and was excommunicated. He attempted to test Joseph's prophetic gift by secretly preparing questions, which Joseph answered by revelation.

name: John C. Bennett
bio: Assistant president of the Church and first mayor of Nauvoo who was excommunicated for immoral conduct in 1842. He subsequently became a vocal critic of the Church. He is referenced in D&C 124.

name: Elias
bio: In LDS theology, a distinct figure from Elijah who appeared in the Kirtland Temple to commit the dispensation of the gospel of Abraham (D&C 110:12). The name 'Elias' is also used as a title meaning 'forerunner' and as the Greek form of Elijah, creating some complexity in scriptural usage.

name: Nephi
aliases: Nephi, son of Nephi, son of Helaman
bio: Son of Nephi (son of Helaman) who was given charge of the sacred records just before Christ's birth. He witnessed the signs of Christ's birth and death. He departed out of the land and was not heard of again. His son, also named Nephi, was among the twelve disciples chosen by Jesus.

name: Timothy
bio: Brother of Nephi (3 Nephi) and one of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus during his visit to the Americas. He was raised from the dead by his brother Nephi after being killed.

name: Jonas
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas following his resurrection.

name: Mathoni
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas. His brother Mathonihah was also among the twelve.

name: Mathonihah
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his appearance to the Nephites. He was the brother of Mathoni.

name: Kumen
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas following his resurrection.

name: Kumenonhi
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his appearance to the Nephites after his resurrection.

name: Jeremiah
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas. He shares the name with the Old Testament prophet but is a distinct individual.

name: Shemnon
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his appearance to the Nephites after his resurrection.

name: Zedekiah
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas. He shares the name with the last king of Judah but is a distinct individual.

name: Isaiah
bio: One of the twelve Nephite disciples chosen by Jesus Christ during his visit to the Americas. He shares the name with the Old Testament prophet but is a distinct individual.

name: Sherem
bio: The first anti-Christ figure in the Book of Mormon who came among the Nephites denying the doctrine of Christ. He confronted Jacob (son of Lehi) and demanded a sign. He was struck down by God, confessed he had been deceived by the devil, and died.

name: Dinah
bio: Daughter of Jacob and Leah who was violated by Shechem, a Hivite prince. Her brothers Simeon and Levi avenged her by deceiving and massacring the men of Shechem, an act that Jacob condemned.

name: Simeon
